rationales.hedonism: He ate 'lunch ... at my desk' until the soup was 'lukewarm' and cut short balcony time to confirm maintenance, repeatedly letting work override simple enjoyment.
rationales.benevolence: He approved the supervisor's December leave because 'he hasn't taken time in months,' prioritizing a team member's wellbeing over staffing convenience.

rationales.hedonism: He 'just wanted to sit and drink tea' but let thoughts about covering shifts make it so 'the tea doesn't taste like anything anymore,' turning rest into self-denial.
rationales.achievement: He couldn't let himself sit still without feeling 'I wasn't being productive,' showing work output was the standard he kept applying even late at night.

rationales.hedonism: Even the small pleasure of tea got abandoned: he 'never actually finished the tea' and found it 'cold on the railing,' suggesting enjoyment was again pushed aside.
He deliberately stayed in the car to eat 'knafeh ... still warm' and notes he 'didn't check my phone once,' fully protecting a moment of enjoyment.
He was 'genuinely tired' and considered leaving early, but stayed, answered emails 'that could have waited,' and ate dinner 'standing in the kitchen' instead of resting.
rationales.conformity: He kept working because of 'how that would look, leaving at three when my team is there until five,' letting role expectations override his own needs.
rationales.benevolence: He treats his own rest as secondary because 'if I rest and something goes wrong, it's my fault' and 'if they rest, I'm the one who covered,' absorbing the burden for his team.

rationales.hedonism: At a gathering with 'good food and music' he spent the whole time thinking about Monday's deadline and 'left early,' missing the enjoyment he had anticipated.
rationales.achievement: A 'procurement deadline on Monday' dominated his attention so completely that it pulled him out of a family event, showing work demands took priority over leisure.

rationales.self_direction: He recoils from the brand brief as 'their story, not mine,' showing a clear pull toward making work on his own terms rather than executing someone else's agenda.
rationales.security: He says he 'should probably say yes because I need the money' but is still pulled toward refusing it, showing willingness to sacrifice stability for integrity.
rationales.universalism: The Syrian girl's sketchbook and not knowing if her grandmother is alive reshape the whole day for him, and he keeps questioning whether his art should do more for people beyond himself.

rationales.self_direction: He says no to Pieter and feels 'RELIEVED,' then starts imagining work that tells 'THEIR story instead of a brand's story,' choosing his own artistic direction.
rationales.stimulation: He wants something 'that moved,' says the murals were 'starting to feel like cages,' and is actively drawn to a new medium that breathes and changes.
rationales.achievement: He'd 'rather be broke and making something real than comfortable and decorating,' explicitly rejecting conventional career success in favor of meaning.
rationales.security: He knows there is 'No money in it probably' and still prefers that path, choosing uncertainty over financial comfort.
rationales.conformity: He brushes off Pieter's disappointment with 'Whatever. I can't be responsible for everyone's disappointment,' refusing to organize his choices around others' approval.
rationales.universalism: He spends the day making art with refugee kids and wants a collaborative piece that tells 'their story,' centering people beyond his immediate circle.

rationales.self_direction: He takes the 'very safe' hotel lobby job and says the honest faces series 'can always wait,' suppressing the work he actually wants to make.
rationales.conformity: He keeps the real series hidden because he can already hear relatives criticizing it, and says he'd need 'someone to tell me it's okay,' showing how strongly social approval governs the choice.

rationales.self_direction: He hides a real relationship and gives 'all the correct answers' at lunch, choosing concealment over speaking for himself.
rationales.conformity: He has become 'very efficient at predicting which battles aren't worth starting,' carefully managing family expectations to keep the room smooth.

rationales.self_direction: He admits the faces series is 'the best work I've ever done' but still keeps it unseen, letting avoidance override the art he actually believes in.
rationales.stimulation: He turns down a 'boundary-pushing' Beirut show and stays with 'technically proficient and completely forgettable' commercial work instead of challenge or novelty.
rationales.conformity: He says every option involves 'disappointing someone or drawing attention,' so he keeps choosing the safer, more socially acceptable path.
He says 'Of course I'll be there' and imagines wearing 'the right shirt' and saying 'the right things,' showing active alignment with family expectations and social scripts.
He smiles through the questions 'because that's what you do' and feels relieved when his aunt calls him 'a good boy,' showing how approval still organizes his behavior.
He refuses the Beirut show because he'd 'rather keep things smooth' and wants to avoid 'the shift in how people look at you when you stop being predictable,' prioritizing social ease over expression.

rationales.self_direction: He 'almost posted' the faces series, then imagines family reactions and 'covered everything back up,' pulling back from his own work at the last second.
rationales.conformity: He says the 'approval, the ease, the feeling of fitting in' are what he is afraid to lose, showing how strongly belonging still dictates his choices.

rationales.self_direction: He adds the tiny figures that feel true to him and then 'painted over them before anyone could see,' again erasing his own artistic impulse.
rationales.conformity: He still calls the faces series 'nothing, just experiments' and says showing it to family depends on finding someone who won't make him feel 'wrong,' showing continued deference to family judgment.

rationales.self_direction: Hearing the client say it was 'exactly what he envisioned' feels 'depressing' because the finished work fully serves someone else's vision rather than his own.
rationales.conformity: The same line shows he successfully matched the client's expectations, even though doing so left him feeling flattened.

rationales.self_direction: He finally showed his sister the faces pieces and says he may be 'getting tired of hiding the work,' a concrete step toward backing his own artistic voice.
rationales.conformity: Leaving the work uncovered and sharing it despite expecting the usual 'concern' or warning about Baba shows him loosening the habit of keeping others comfortable first.
